topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the ballroom was thick with laughter and the clinking of champagne flutes. I was deep in conversation with Sarah, trying to appear engaged, when a familiar silhouette caught my eye across the crowded room. It was him. Mark. He was standing near the cake table, looking impossibly polished in a suit, and for a split second, the music seemed to mute. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp constriction in my chest. I felt my carefully constructed composure begin to fray at the edges. I deliberately shifted my weight, focusing intently on a nearby floral arrangement, trying to anchor myself back to the present moment. When he turned his head, our eyes met. The casual warmth that usually flickered between us was replaced by a startling, heavy silence that seemed to stretch out across the expanse of the room. I managed a tight, brittle smile, offering a small nod, and then quickly turned my attention back to Sarah, my hands suddenly feeling clammy.

[story 2]

I was nursing a lukewarm drink near the edge of the dance floor, watching the proceedings with a detached sort of amusement. Then, the entrance. He walked in, and the noise level around him seemed to dip, as if everyone was holding their breath. It was Mark. The sight of him, looking effortlessly charming, sent a strange tremor through my limbs. My fingers tightened around my glass until my knuckles were white. I watched him navigate the crowd, and every movement he made felt amplified, scrutinized under the glare of the overhead lights. When his gaze swept across the room and snagged on mine, a distinct coldness washed over me. I felt an immediate, unwelcome urge to retreat, to melt into the shadows. I took a long, slow sip of my drink, trying to regulate the rapid pulse hammering against my ribs, my jaw feeling strangely locked.

[story 3]

The noise of the reception was overwhelming—a vibrant, chaotic symphony of celebration. I was trying to keep pace with a group of acquaintances when I saw him. He was standing near the bar, laughing with someone, and the sound of his voice, even from across the room, hit me with unexpected force. My focus shattered. I stopped mid-stride, my mind momentarily blanking. A flush crept up my neck, spreading across my cheeks, and I instinctively pulled my jacket tighter around myself, a purely defensive gesture. When he finally looked up and saw me, the easy smile he wore faltered, replaced by an expression I couldn't decipher, but it was undeniably charged. I mumbled something to a nearby friend, my voice sounding unnaturally high, and then abruptly turned away, needing distance more than I needed a drink.
